metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the equivalence-test NDJSON fixture (AC4) to include at least one ticket with a parent relationship and one with a file-impact/verify-command, so that Passes 2a and 2c each carry ≥1 event through the batch path and AC3 is fully exercised.
findings.finding: The equivalence test NDJSON fixture is never specified to include parent-assignment or file-impact/verify-command data, so the batch path for Passes 2a and 2c is never exercised by any AC and AC3's claim that those passes 'run under batch_sink(buf)' has no proving command.
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, change 'imports the same NDJSON' to 'imports the same NDJSON, which MUST include at least one parent-child relationship (exercises Pass 2a) and at least one file-impact or verify-command entry (exercises Pass 2c), so every batched pass carries ≥1 event through the batch_sink path'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or test) that simulates a flush failure in `batch_stage_and_commit` (e.g., by patching it to raise) and asserts the import raises/aborts rather than silently skipping the chunk — verifying the stated all-or-nothing guarantee.
findings.finding: The plan states a behavioral guarantee — 'a git-level flush failure aborts the import with a clear error' — but no AC tests this failure path. The all-or-nothing flush semantics are a new behavior introduced by the batch mechanism, and they have no corresponding acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Flush-boundary failure semantics section; Acceptance Criteria (all ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'Flush-failure test: patch `batch_stage_and_commit` to raise `RuntimeError` on the second call; assert the importer propagates the exception (does not warn-and-continue), and that `rev-list --count HEAD` shows only the events from the first (successful) flush chunk were committed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In AC4, provide a concrete `_normalize` skeleton that names the exact key paths to strip (e.g., top-level keys and the keys inside each element of the `events` list), or add a note that the executor should confirm the exact structure from `rebar.show_ticket` and document it inline in the test helper.
findings.finding: The `_normalize` helper in AC4 is specified only as 'deleting the volatile keys (created_at, updated_at, env_id, and every event/comment uuid+timestamp)' without stating the key paths inside the nested dict returned by `rebar.show_ticket`; an executor who implements the normalizer at the wrong nesting level (e.g., top-level only, missing nested event list entries) will produce a test that either always fails (spurious volatile key mismatch) or masks real divergences.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC4 with: '_normalize strips: top-level keys `created_at`, `updated_at`, `env_id`; and within each element of `show_ticket()["events"]` (if present) the keys `uuid` and `timestamp`. If show_ticket nests comments separately under a `comments` key, also strip `uuid` and `timestamp` from each comment element.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend the 'one sink' premise in the 'Deferred-commit sink' section to acknowledge that `txn.transition_core` (status/claim writes) bypasses `append_event` and commits inline — clarify that the contextvar's scope covers only `append_event`-routed writes, and that Pass 2e's exclusion from batching is both correct AND necessary because status writes are invisible to the contextvar.
findings.finding: The plan's foundational premise that 'every write ultimately calls the ONE sink `_seam.append_event`' is refuted by `txn.transition_core` and `claim_core`, which explicitly bypass `append_event` and commit directly under their own lock. This is documented in `txn.py` lines 21-23: 'This still does NOT use `rebar._store.event_append.stage_and_commit`/`write_and_push`'. While Pass 2e (statuses) is correctly excluded from batching, the plan's architectural premise—used to justify 'guardrail 1 is structurally guaranteed'—is incomplete: a future developer reading the plan's 'one sink' claim may incorrectly believe the contextvar intercepts ALL writes, when status/claim writes are invisible to it.
findings.location: ## Deferred-commit sink section — 'every write...ultimately calls the ONE sink `_seam.append_event`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note: 'Note: `txn.transition_core` (used by Pass 2e status transitions) and `claim_core` do NOT route through `append_event` — they commit inline under their own lock. The `_batch_sink` contextvar has no effect on those writes. Pass 2e is therefore per-event by structural necessity, not just by design choice.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `batch_sink()` uses `try/finally` (or equivalent `__exit__` exception handling) to unconditionally reset `_batch_sink` to its prior value on exit, even when an exception propagates out of the wrapped block
findings.finding: The `batch_sink()` contextmanager's reset behavior on exception exit is not specified. If it does not use `try/finally` to reset `_batch_sink` to its prior value (None), an exception during a batched pass (e.g., a crash mid-Pass 1) will leave the contextvar set to a stale buffer. Any subsequent interactive write in the same process (e.g., a `create` or `comment` call) would silently buffer instead of committing, violating Guardrail 1 and causing data loss with no error surfaced.
findings.suggested_fix:
Implement `batch_sink` as:
```python
@contextlib.contextmanager
def batch_sink(buffer: list):
    token = _batch_sink.set(buffer)
    try:
        yield buffer
    finally:
        _batch_sink.reset(token)
```
Using `ContextVar.set()` which returns a `Token`, and `ContextVar.reset(token)` in `finally` restores the prior value atomically regardless of exception.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 'same observable behavior as today' claim with the all-or-nothing flush: either retract the equivalence claim and document the new chunk-level failure mode explicitly, or describe a recovery mechanism (e.g., per-chunk warn-and-continue) that actually preserves fail-open row-granularity semantics.
findings.finding: The 'Flush-boundary failure semantics' section claims 'same observable behavior as today' for row-level failures, but simultaneously introduces an all-or-nothing batch flush that creates a new failure mode absent from the current design: a git-level flush failure silently drops all successfully-composed rows in the current chunk (they were never committed and get no individual warn()), whereas today each row is independently committed-or-warned. These two claims contradict each other across sections.
findings.location: Flush-boundary failure semantics section vs. Deferred-commit sink section (mechanism description)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'same observable behavior as today' with: 'Row-level COMPOSITION failures (bad field, validation) are warn()-skipped as today. However, flush-time git failures are a new chunk-level failure mode: all composed-but-unflushed rows in the chunk are abandoned and will be re-processed on the next idempotent re-run. This is a deliberate trade-off: flush failures are expected to be rare and transient, and the idempotent re-run guarantee makes this safe.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `tests/` entry to the Scope section listing the new test file(s) required by AC2 (guardrail unit test), AC4 (equivalence pytest), AC5 (benchmark), and AC6 (idempotent re-import test) so the edit surface matches the full set of deliverables.
findings.finding: The 'Scope (edit surface)' section lists only two production source files (`_seam.py`, `import_ndjson.py`) as the full edit surface, but AC2, AC4, AC5, and AC6 each require new test files that are not mentioned anywhere in Scope. An implementer using Scope as a work-breakdown checklist would have no indication that new test files are a deliverable.
findings.location: Scope section vs. AC2, AC4, AC5, AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: '- `tests/test_import_ndjson_batch.py` (new) — contains the guardrail unit test (AC2), equivalence pytest (AC4), N=3000 benchmark (AC5), and idempotent re-import test (AC6).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md note documenting the ContextVar-based ambient commit-deferral pattern: why it was chosen over kwarg threading, what invariants callers must uphold (only importers set the var, interactive writes must never set it, buffered passes must not read committed state mid-pass), and which passes are excluded and why.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — a module-level `contextvars.ContextVar` that silently alters commit behavior based on ambient context — with no mention of capturing this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design-doc update. Future maintainers (or future passes of the importer) have no documented guidance on why this pattern was chosen over alternatives (kwarg threading, a strategy object, a separate write-path), what invariants must be preserved, or which passes must NOT set the contextvar.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR (or a `## Architecture Note` block in CLAUDE.md) covering: (1) the single-sink ContextVar pattern and its rationale, (2) the contract that only batch-safe, append-only passes may set `_batch_sink`, (3) the TOCTOU reason Pass 2b/2e are excluded, and (4) the 256-chunk default rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a brief comment in `_seam.py` / `import_ndjson.py` explaining why 256 was chosen (e.g., 'empirically chosen: balances git object-pack size vs. memory on a standard dev machine; tune if fsync latency dominates'), OR expose it as a module-level constant (not a config key) with a docstring so future tuning has a documented starting point.
findings.finding: The batch chunk size of 256 is hard-coded with the plan explicitly rejecting configurability ('hard-coded — no new config key'). While 256 is a reasonable engineering choice, no rationale is given for why this value is stable across different environments (e.g., storage backends with different fsync costs, CI vs. production, memory-constrained runners). Future tuning requires a code change with no documented derivation to guide it.
findings.location: Plan section: 'Scope (edit surface)' — 'flush `buf` through `batch_stage_and_commit` in chunks (default 256, hard-coded — no new config key)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a named constant `_BATCH_CHUNK_SIZE = 256` at the top of `import_ndjson.py` with a comment: '# Empirically chosen; each chunk is one git commit. Increase for faster imports on fast storage, decrease if per-flush memory is a concern.' This costs nothing and makes future tuning discoverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-condition check to the B2 AC (or the implementation scope section) that verifies `batch_stage_and_commit` is importable from `rebar._store.event_append` before B2 work begins — e.g., `python -c 'from rebar._store.event_append import batch_stage_and_commit'` as a gate in the implementation steps.
findings.finding: `batch_stage_and_commit` (the B1 primitive this plan's entire batching mechanism depends on) does not exist in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` at the current HEAD. A grep across all of `src/rebar/_store` returns no matches. The B1 ticket (890d-4733-1c88-4976) is `in_progress` and its plan-review signature explicitly records `dep absent tests/unit/test_store_batch_write.py`, confirming the implementation was not merged when B1's plan was reviewed. The plan calls B1 a 'hard prerequisite' but does not verify or enforce that B1 is actually merged before B2 work begins.
findings.location: ## What — 'hard prerequisite; this ticket depends_on B1'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Pre-condition: confirm B1 is merged by running `python -c "from rebar._store.event_append import batch_stage_and_commit, batch_write_and_push"` — if this fails, B1 is not yet available and B2 cannot proceed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all direct callers of `append_event` outside `_commands/` (at minimum: `signing.py`, `llm/plan_review/sidecar.py`, `llm/code_review/sidecar.py`, `llm/workflow/recorder.py`, `_lib_writes.py::attach_commits`) and confirm in the plan body that each is unreachable while `_batch_sink` is set, or add a guard in `batch_sink()` that asserts no concurrent sink is active in other callsites.
findings.finding: The plan's 'Scope' and 'Why' sections enumerate only the callers inside `_commands/` and the importer as affected by the `_batch_sink` contextvar, but at least four additional direct callers of `append_event` exist outside `_commands/` and outside the importer: `src/rebar/signing.py:517`, `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py:56`, `src/rebar/llm/code_review/sidecar.py:122`, `src/rebar/llm/workflow/recorder.py:89`, and `src/rebar/_lib_writes.py:487` (`attach_commits`). The plan does not enumerate these callers to confirm they are never triggered while the contextvar is set — if any were called during a batched import pass (e.g., a hook or callback triggered by `create_ticket`), they would silently buffer their events instead of committing.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the 'Deferred-commit sink' section explicitly listing these out-of-`_commands` callers and confirming they are not invoked by `create_ticket`/`edit_ticket`/`comment`/`set_file_impact`/`set_verify_commands` call chains. If any doubt exists, the `batch_sink()` context manager can log a warning or assert that the import is not running signing/sidecar hooks by checking a secondary sentinel.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC (or a note in Scope) codifying the flush-before-next-pass ordering as a correctness requirement: 'each pass MUST call batch_stage_and_commit to completion before the next pass begins, because within-pass guards (require_not_ghost, require_id) read committed disk state.'
findings.finding: The between-pass flush is a load-bearing ordering invariant for within-pass guard correctness (require_not_ghost, require_id, _is_active_link-style disk reads), but the plan states it only briefly ('each pass's buffer is flushed (committed) before the next') without codifying it as a formal precondition in the AC or Scope. If an implementer reorders passes or adds a new batched pass that reads prior-pass state before flushing, the guards silently produce stale results — the plan provides no contract that would catch this.
findings.location: Scope section ('Passes run sequentially and each pass's buffer is flushed (committed) before the next') and Acceptance Criteria (AC item 3)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC item 3: 'The implementation MUST flush each pass's buffer to completion (all chunks committed) before beginning the next pass; this is required for correctness of within-pass disk-reading guards (require_not_ghost, require_id).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or a pre-flight note) that verifies `batch_stage_and_commit` and `batch_write_and_push` are importable from `rebar._store.event_append` with the exact call signature B2 uses (e.g., `batch_stage_and_commit(tracker, items)`) before B2 implementation begins — a one-line smoke import in the benchmark test or a `# requires B1 merged` assertion guard is sufficient.
findings.finding: `batch_stage_and_commit` (and `batch_write_and_push`) — the flush primitive B2 calls at every pass boundary — does not exist in `src/rebar/_store/event_append.py` at current HEAD. B1 (ticket 890d-4733-1c88-4976) is `in_progress` and its plan-review manifest records the test file as `dep absent`, confirming the implementation is not yet merged. The plan asserts this capability as callable throughout its AC and mechanism section but provides no AC-level integration-verification step (e.g., confirming the function is importable with the expected signature before B2 work starts). A signature mismatch between what B1 ships and what B2 calls would produce a runtime `AttributeError` or `TypeError` discovered only at test time.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC3 / 'Deferred-commit sink' section / 'Flush-boundary failure semantics' section
findings.suggested_fix: In the benchmark test (AC5), add `from rebar._store.event_append import batch_stage_and_commit, batch_write_and_push` at the top of the test module (not inside a function) so a missing or renamed function fails immediately at collection time with a clear `ImportError`, not silently mid-run.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add instrumentation to the batch flush path: log (at INFO level minimum) the pass name, total events buffered, number of warn()-skipped rows, number of chunks flushed, and any flush error with its chunk index so operators can monitor and debug batch imports
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability instrumentation for the batch flush operation itself — no log lines, metrics, or traces for flush start/completion, event counts per chunk, timing, or flush failures. Operators cannot distinguish a slow/hung flush from a slow import, and cannot audit how many events were batched or dropped (warn()-skipped) per pass.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
